title: स्पेन और फ्रांस में जंगल की आग और भीषण गर्मी की लहरों का सामना करना पड़ रहा है। - Vietnam.vn
search_term: (लू OR "लू चलना" OR "भीषण गर्मी" OR "प्रचंड गर्मी" OR "कड़ी धूप" OR "तेज धूप" OR "गर्म हवा" OR "उष्ण लहर" OR "ताप लहर" OR "हीट वेव" OR "झुलसाने वाली गर्मी" OR "तपती गर्मी" OR "भयंकर गर्मी" OR "गर्मी का कहर" OR "गर्मी का प्रकोप" OR "अत्यधिक गर्मी" OR मौसम OR "मौसम अपडेट" OR "मौसम का हाल" OR "मौसम पूर्वानुमान" OR "मौसम अलर्ट" OR आईएमडी OR "आईएमडी पूर्वानुमान" OR "आईएमडी अलर्ट" OR "तापमान में बढ़ोतरी" OR "गर्मी से राहत" OR "गर्मी से निजात") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
फ्रांस में भीषण गर्मी और पानी की कमी के बाद बिगड़ते सूखे की स्थिति के बीच गिरोंडे प्रांत के एक वन क्षेत्र में भीषण आग लग गई। स्रोत: रॉयटर्स।
आंकड़ों से पता चलता है कि इस वर्ष यूरोप में जंगल की आग ने पिछले दो दशकों के वार्षिक औसत की तुलना में अधिक भूमि को जला दिया है, क्योंकि दुनिया के सबसे तेजी से गर्म होने वाले महाद्वीप ने इस वर्ष लगभग लगातार तीन भीषण लू का सामना किया है।
फ्रांस में, अधिकारियों ने लोगों से लू से प्रभावित क्षेत्रों में बाहरी ग्रिल का उपयोग करने से बचने का आग्रह किया है, इस बात पर जोर देते हुए कि 10 में से 9 जंगल की आग (चाहे आकस्मिक हो या जानबूझकर) मानवीय गतिविधि के कारण शुरू होती है।
पश्चिमी बाल्कन में, खतरा लू से हटकर ओलावृष्टि में बदल गया है, जिससे उत्तरी मैसेडोनिया और पूर्वी कोसोवो में कई लोग घायल हो गए हैं।
वैज्ञानिकों का कहना है कि मानव जनित जलवायु परिवर्तन से लू, सूखे और जंगल की आग की तीव्रता बढ़ रही है, साथ ही अचानक और विनाशकारी तूफानों के लिए भी परिस्थितियां पैदा हो रही हैं।
रॉयटर्स के जलवायु निगरानी उपकरण, जो वास्तविक समय का डेटा प्रदान करने वाला एक इंटरैक्टिव उपकरण है, से पता चलता है कि पश्चिमी यूरोप में औसत अधिकतम तापमान 26.6 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है, जो 1961 और 1990 के बीच की अवधि में 22 जुलाई को औसत अधिकतम तापमान से 3 डिग्री अधिक है।
स्पेन में जंगल की आग
इस साल स्पेन में लगी भीषण जंगल की आग ने ग्वाडालाजारा क्षेत्र में अनुमानित 32,000 हेक्टेयर भूमि को जला दिया है। 22 जुलाई को 34 नगरपालिकाओं को खाली करने और 14 अन्य में लॉकडाउन लागू करने के बाद स्थिति स्थिर होने लगी।
कैस्टिला-ला मांचा (न्यू स्पेन) की इन्फोकैम अग्निशमन सेवा ने कहा कि पांच गांवों के निवासियों को वापस लौटने की अनुमति दे दी गई है, हालांकि अन्य प्रतिबंध अभी भी लागू हैं।
23 जुलाई को इस क्षेत्र के दौरे के दौरान, प्रधानमंत्री पेड्रो सांचेज़ ने कहा कि स्पेन में इस साल 22 बड़ी जंगल की आग की घटनाएं हुई हैं। उन्होंने कहा, "जंगल की आग से निपटने के सबसे गंभीर दौर में प्रवेश करने से पहले ही हम 100,000 हेक्टेयर क्षेत्र में आग की चपेट में आ चुके हैं। इसे समझने के लिए, यह पिछले एक दशक में स्पेन में देखी गई औसत वार्षिक आग की घटनाओं का स्तर है।" सांचेज़ ने आगे कहा कि आग को रोकने के लिए और अधिक प्रयास करने की आवश्यकता है।
आपको यह भी पसंद आ सकता है
स्पेन की मौसम विज्ञान एजेंसी एईएमईटी ने कहा कि स्पेन की मुख्य भूमि और बैलेरिक द्वीप समूह के अधिकांश हिस्सों में जंगल की आग का खतरा "बहुत अधिक या अत्यधिक उच्च" बना हुआ है और आने वाले दिनों में इसके और बढ़ने की उम्मीद है।
एजेंसी ने लोगों से मौजूदा लू के साथ शुष्क तूफान और सहारा रेगिस्तान की धूल के संयोजन को देखते हुए "अत्यधिक सावधानी" बरतने का आग्रह किया।
हालांकि 24 जुलाई को कुछ क्षेत्रों में तापमान में थोड़ी गिरावट आएगी, लेकिन शुष्क पश्चिमी हवाओं के कारण भूमध्यसागरीय क्षेत्र में एक नई लू की तीव्रता बढ़ने का अनुमान है, जहां कुछ क्षेत्रों में तापमान 42 से 44 डिग्री सेल्सियस तक पहुंच सकता है।
25 जुलाई से तापमान में काफी गिरावट आने की संभावना है। हालांकि, यह गिरावट अल्पकालिक हो सकती है। पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार, 27 जुलाई से तापमान में फिर से वृद्धि होगी और अगले सप्ताह की शुरुआत में स्पेन भर में भीषण गर्मी लौट सकती है।
फ्रांस के वार प्रांत के कोटिग्नाक इलाके में जंगल की आग से प्रभावित क्षेत्र में एक घर के पास लगी आग को बुझाने के लिए फ्रांस के दमकलकर्मी जूझ रहे हैं। स्रोत: रॉयटर्स।
फ्रांस में जंगल की आग
फ्रांस में, दक्षिण-पश्चिमी गिरोंडे क्षेत्र में लगी आग ने बोर्डो और कैप फेरेट के बीच 100 हेक्टेयर क्षेत्र को तबाह कर दिया, जिससे अधिकारियों को 23 जुलाई को सौमोस गांव के पास 600 लोगों को निकालने और सड़क यातायात को क्षेत्र के लोकप्रिय समुद्र तटों की ओर मोड़ने के लिए मजबूर होना पड़ा।
गिरोंडे क्षेत्र की गवर्नर सोफी ब्रोकास ने पत्रकारों से कहा, "हर जगह बहुत सूखा है; आज भी हमें कई जगहों पर आग लगने की घटनाएं देखने को मिल रही हैं, जिनमें से एक काफी जटिल है। मैं सभी वानिकी गतिविधियों और आंतरिक दहन इंजनों के उपयोग से संबंधित किसी भी गतिविधि पर प्रतिबंध लगाऊंगी।"
आधिकारिक मौसम पूर्वानुमान एजेंसी मेटियो फ्रांस के अनुसार, पश्चिमी और दक्षिण-पश्चिमी फ्रांस में इस सप्ताह के बाकी दिनों के साथ-साथ दक्षिण-पूर्वी फ्रांस में कम से कम 24 जुलाई तक जंगल की आग का खतरा बहुत अधिक बना हुआ है।
स्थानीय अधिकारियों ने कहा कि तापमान में नरमी और हवाओं के कम होने से दक्षिणपूर्वी वार क्षेत्र में 2,500 हेक्टेयर में फैली जंगल की आग को बुझाने के प्रयासों में मदद मिली है, जिससे दर्जनों घर क्षतिग्रस्त हो गए हैं और 400 लोगों को अपना घर छोड़कर सुरक्षित स्थानों पर जाना पड़ा है।
फ्रांस के गृह मंत्री लॉरेंट नुनेज ने गिरोंडे में कहा कि इस साल अब तक फ्रांस में 44,000 हेक्टेयर जमीन जल चुकी है, जो औसत से अधिक है। वह गिरोंडे में उस इलाके में लगी एक अन्य आग से लड़ते हुए जान गंवाने वाले दो दमकलकर्मियों को श्रद्धांजलि देने के लिए गए थे।
अधिकारियों ने बताया कि दक्षिणपूर्वी फ्रांस के हाउतेस-आल्प्स में भी एक और आग भयंकर रूप से फैल रही है।
ग्रीस में तापमान 41 डिग्री सेल्सियस से ऊपर पहुंचने का अनुमान है, जिसके चलते अधिकारियों ने दोपहर की चिलचिलाती धूप में बाहरी काम पर प्रतिबंध लगा दिया है। साइप्रस में भी, जहां गर्मियों में तापमान अधिक रहता है, बुधवार दोपहर को बाहरी काम पर रोक लगा दी गई।
स्थानीय मीडिया रिपोर्टों के अनुसार, उत्तरी मैसेडोनिया की राजधानी स्कोप्जे में 22 जुलाई की रात आए तूफान के बाद 11 लोगों को अस्पताल में भर्ती कराया गया, जिनमें से तीन गंभीर रूप से घायल हैं। तूफान के कारण पेड़ गिर गए, छतों को नुकसान पहुंचा और बाल्कन क्षेत्र में बिजली गुल हो गई। सड़कें अवरुद्ध हो गईं और कुछ कस्बों में बिजली और पानी की आपूर्ति बाधित हो गई।
कोसोवो में, जल मौसम विज्ञान संस्थान ने कहा कि 23 जुलाई को और अधिक तूफान आने की आशंका है और बिजली गिरने, बिजली ग्रिड को नुकसान, ब्लैकआउट, बाढ़ और संपत्ति के नुकसान की संभावना के बारे में चेतावनी दी है।
माई फुओंग

title: एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह छाए रहेंगे काले बादल, हल्की बारिश से सुहावना रहेगा मौसम - bhaskarhindi.com
search_term: ("पारा चढ़ा" OR "पारा पहुंचा" OR तापमान OR "तापमान बढ़ा" OR "रिकॉर्ड तोड़ गर्मी" OR "डिग्री सेल्सियस" OR "पारा उबला" OR "अधिकतम तापमान" OR "तापमान 40 पार" OR "तापमान 45 पार" OR "तापमान बढ़ने की संभावना") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
एनसीआर में पूरे सप्ताह छाए रहेंगे काले बादल, हल्की बारिश से सुहावना रहेगा मौसम
By - |23 July 2026 9:48 AM IST
राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) के लोगों के लिए मौसम विभाग की ओर से राहत भरी खबर है। भारतीय मौसम विभाग (आईएमडी) के ताजा पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार पूरे सप्ताह एनसीआर में मौसम सुहावना बना रहेगा। आसमान पर काले और घने बादलों का डेरा रहेगा तथा बीच-बीच में हल्की बारिश और बूंदाबांदी लोगों को गर्मी और उमस से राहत दिलाएगी।
नोएडा, 23 जुलाई (आईएएनएस)। राष्ट्रीय राजधानी क्षेत्र (एनसीआर) के लोगों के लिए मौसम विभाग की ओर से राहत भरी खबर है। भारतीय मौसम विभाग (आईएमडी) के ताजा पूर्वानुमान के अनुसार पूरे सप्ताह एनसीआर में मौसम सुहावना बना रहेगा। आसमान पर काले और घने बादलों का डेरा रहेगा तथा बीच-बीच में हल्की बारिश और बूंदाबांदी लोगों को गर्मी और उमस से राहत दिलाएगी।
खास बात यह है कि इस अवधि के लिए कोई भी मौसम संबंधी चेतावनी जारी नहीं की गई है, जिससे सामान्य जनजीवन पर किसी बड़े प्रभाव की आशंका नहीं है।
मौसम विभाग के पूर्वानुमान के मुताबिक, 23 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री सेल्सियस और न्यूनतम 24 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। इस दिन अधिकतम आर्द्रता 95 प्रतिशत और न्यूनतम 80 प्रतिशत रहेगी। दिनभर आसमान में बादलों की आवाजाही बनी रहेगी तथा हल्की बारिश होने की संभावना है।
24 जुलाई को भी मौसम लगभग इसी तरह बना रहेगा। अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने की संभावना है। इस दिन मौसम विभाग ने हल्की बारिश या बूंदाबांदी का पूर्वानुमान जारी किया है। इसके बाद 25 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 26 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। इस दिन सामान्य रूप से बादल छाए रहने की संभावना है, जबकि भारी बारिश की कोई चेतावनी नहीं है।
26 जुलाई को तापमान में हल्की बढ़ोतरी देखने को मिल सकती है। अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 26 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। पूरे दिन बादल छाए रहेंगे और मौसम सामान्य रहेगा। 27 जुलाई को भी अधिकतम तापमान 34 डिग्री तथा न्यूनतम 25 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का पूर्वानुमान है। आर्द्रता 80 से 70 प्रतिशत के बीच रहने की संभावना है। दिनभर आसमान में बादल छाए रहेंगे और मौसम आरामदायक बना रहेगा।
वहीं, 28 जुलाई को अधिकतम तापमान 33 डिग्री और न्यूनतम 24 डिग्री सेल्सियस रहने का अनुमान है। इस दिन भी आसमान में बादलों का डेरा रहेगा और मौसम विभाग ने किसी प्रकार की चेतावनी जारी नहीं की है।
मौसम विशेषज्ञों का कहना है कि सप्ताहभर बादलों की लगातार आवाजाही और बीच-बीच में होने वाली हल्की बारिश के कारण भीषण गर्मी से राहत मिलेगी। हालांकि, अधिक आर्द्रता के कारण कुछ समय के लिए उमस महसूस हो सकती है। फिर भी तेज बारिश, आंधी या किसी अन्य गंभीर मौसम संबंधी स्थिति की फिलहाल संभावना नहीं जताई गई है।
फिलहाल आने वाला पूरा सप्ताह एनसीआर के लोगों के लिए राहतभरा रहने वाला है। काले बादलों की मौजूदगी, हल्की बारिश की फुहारें और सामान्य तापमान के चलते मौसम खुशनुमा बना रहेगा। मौसम विभाग ने पूरे सप्ताह के लिए 'नो वार्निंग' जारी रखी है, जिससे लोगों को मौसम को लेकर अधिक चिंता करने की आवश्यकता नहीं है।
अस्वीकरण: यह न्यूज़ ऑटो फ़ीड्स द्वारा स्वतः प्रकाशित हुई खबर है। इस न्यूज़ में BhaskarHindi.com टीम के द्वारा किसी भी तरह का कोई बदलाव या परिवर्तन (एडिटिंग) नहीं किया गया है| इस न्यूज की एवं न्यूज में उपयोग में ली गई सामग्रियों की सम्पूर्ण जवाबदारी केवल और केवल न्यूज़ एजेंसी की है एवं इस न्यूज में दी गई जानकारी का उपयोग करने से पहले संबंधित क्षेत्र के विशेषज्ञों (वकील / इंजीनियर / ज्योतिष / वास्तुशास्त्री / डॉक्टर / न्यूज़ एजेंसी / अन्य विषय एक्सपर्ट) की सलाह जरूर लें। अतः संबंधित खबर एवं उपयोग में लिए गए टेक्स्ट मैटर, फोटो, विडियो एवं ऑडिओ को लेकर BhaskarHindi.com न्यूज पोर्टल की कोई भी जिम्मेदारी नहीं है|

title: Extreme heat raises risk of low-birth-weight, preterm babies in Telangana: Study - The New Indian Express
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
HYDERABAD: Pregnant women in Telangana may face a higher risk of delivering low birth-weight or preterm babies after exposure to just a few days of extreme heat, according to a new national-level study. Health experts have therefore called for expectant mothers to be recognised as a priority group in the State’s Heat Action Plan.
The study also found that neighbouring Andhra Pradesh could experience a similar rise in the risk of low-birth-weight births following short spells of extreme heat, highlighting the growing public health challenge posed by rising temperatures across the two Telugu states.
The research was led by the University of Queensland-IIT Delhi Academy of Research in collaboration with IIT Delhi, the University of Queensland’s School of Public Health, UNSW Sydney, the University of Exeter and Korea University.
Published in the peer-reviewed journal Environmental Research Communications, the study found that three to four consecutive “sweltering” days during pregnancy were enough to significantly increase the risk of adverse birth outcomes. Researchers analysed 2,09,266 births recorded across India between 2015 and 2020 using data from the National Family Health Survey (NFHS-5). Each pregnancy was matched with local heat exposure levels using the India Heat Index, which factors in temperature, humidity, wind speed and solar radiation.
The researchers found that every additional consecutive sweltering day during pregnancy increased the odds of low birth weight by about 1 per cent. Heat exposure during the first trimester was linked to a higher risk of preterm birth, while exposure during the second trimester was associated with an increased likelihood of low birth weight.
The study also found that women with little or no formal education, those from economically weaker households and mothers with a low body mass index were more vulnerable to the effects of extreme heat during pregnancy.
Lead author Vineetha Vincent said that although the increase in risk per day appeared small, the cumulative impact of prolonged heat exposure during pregnancy could become a significant public health concern.
Co-author Prof Sagnik Dey of IIT Delhi said adaptation measures should prioritise pregnant women, particularly those from economically and socially disadvantaged backgrounds.
Health experts pointed out that the findings are especially significant for Telangana and Andhra Pradesh, where summer temperatures frequently cross 40°C and heatwave alerts have become increasingly common.
Senior gynaecologists described the study as one of the first large-scale Indian investigations to directly examine the impact of climate change on pregnancy outcomes. They said the findings highlight an overlooked public health concern and underline the need for targeted interventions to protect expectant mothers. Experts also noted that women from economically weaker sections are likely to face the greatest risks, as many continue to work outdoors during pregnancy and have limited access to cooling facilities.
Obstetricians advised pregnant women to stay well hydrated, avoid outdoor activities during peak afternoon hours, attend regular antenatal check-ups and seek immediate medical attention if they experience dizziness, dehydration or reduced foetal movements during periods of extreme heat.
They added that the findings should serve as a wake-up call for the Telangana and Andhra Pradesh governments to integrate maternal health into their Heat Action Plans, strengthen awareness campaigns and issue specific heat advisories for pregnant women before and during the summer season.

title: The June Heat Wave Cost The U.K. Economy Over $1.5 Billion - Time Magazine
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Last month’s heat wave in the U.K. not only broke June temperature records but may have cost the country more than £1 billion ($1.33 billion) in lost economic output. That’s according to a new analysis by the Grantham Research Institute on Climate Change and the Environment and the Euro-Mediterranean Center on Climate Change (CMCC).
The heat wave, which lasted between June 18 and July 1, resulted in England’s warmest June since records began in 1884, with daily maximum temperatures often reaching over 90°F. A provisional high of 37.7°C (99.86°F) was recorded at Lingwood, Strumpshaw Hill in Norfolk on June 26, the most significant new national record for June. High temperatures led to delayed and cancelled trains, while shops, offices, and schools were forced to close.
Elizabeth Robinson, director of the Grantham Research Institute, and Shouro Dasgupta, environmental economist at the Euro-Mediterranean Center on Climate Change and visiting senior fellow at the Grantham Research Institute, carried out a survey to better understand how the heat affected workers around the country during the week of June 22. They then scaled that information to calculate the cost to the economy.
The two researchers surveyed 1,950 U.K. adults across sectors, asking them the perceived impact the heat wave had on a number of factors—including working hours, effort while working, health, ability to sleep, changes to how they got to work or their place of work, and their working environment. They were then able to estimate the economic cost imposed by the heat wave in terms of lost output: approximately £1.15 billion (over $1.5 billion) of lost output in a single week.
The figure is likely higher, says Robinson. “We don't include whether people have reduced their effort, [as in] the productivity per hour, because you can't measure that with the approach we take,” she notes. “So it gives us quite a conservative estimate of the economic costs of the heat wave.
According to the survey, the average reported reduction in hours worked across the week of the heat wave was 0.47 hours—which would suggest approximately 16 million lost hours in a working population of 34.4 million. Those working in physically demanding jobs or those exposed to heat, like construction and agriculture, reduced their hours by more than those working in low-exposure and physically undemanding jobs such as office workers. About 3.6% of respondents, equivalent to approximately 1.25 million workers in the population as a whole, reported that they did not work at all that week because of the heat.
Many respondents reported a number of adaptations to deal with the heat—be it changing the hours they worked, the effort they put into working, where they work, or their commute. Forty-nine percent of workers said they had access to air conditioning, while 27% said their employers made changes to the work environment to cool it down—like installing A.C., increasing ventilation, or providing shade.
“We've got very clear evidence that adaptation measures do successfully reduce how many hours are lost to heat, and adaptation measures do successfully protect workers' health to some extent,” says Robinson.
Even still, the findings point to a country unprepared for heat waves—which are only expected to grow more frequent and intense with climate change. The U.K. is particularly vulnerable when it comes to heat waves. Much of the country’s housing stock is old, and built for a much cooler climate. Buildings were designed to keep heat in, turning them into furnaces during the now more frequently occurring heat waves. “The U.K. is built for a country that's cold and not too hot, and now we're having [what feels like] Mediterranean weather with buildings that are built to keep us warm, not to cool us down,” says Robinson.
“We are woefully under-adapted to the changing climate,” says Robinson. “[This survey] provides evidence for governments and employers that it makes sense to invest in adaptations because the frequency of heat waves [and] the intensity is only going to get worse.”
Dasgupta also hopes that the findings encourage governments to create regulations around heat and working environments to ensure that heat adaptation becomes more widespread. “We need regulations in terms of either maximum temperature thresholds, mandatory rest breaks, or additional hydration breaks,” he says. “The effects are right now too significant for this to be left alone.”

title: Extreme heat in Central Asia - AsiaNews
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
Bishkek (AsiaNews) – An unusual heatwave has hit the countries of Central Asia. In Tajikistan, residents are reporting power cuts, whilst in Kyrgyzstan electricity is being temporarily cut off in some areas due to increased consumption. In Uzbekistan, the authorities have ordered the suspension of hazardous outdoor work during the hottest hours of the day. In Kazakhstan, high temperatures have caused road surfaces to warp. In this region, which is particularly vulnerable to climate change, all temperature records have been broken.
From 17 to 22 July, the highest temperatures in Tajikistan were recorded in the low-lying areas of the Khatlon region.
In Šakhristan province, the air temperature reached 46 degrees, and forecasts for the coming days in Bokhtar and Kulyab indicate 48 degrees. In Dushanbe, the maximum temperature hovered around 43 degrees, and meteorologists are forecasting a peak of 44 degrees. Since mid-July, new record high temperatures have been recorded in many cities across Central Asia, in some cases exceeding 50 degrees.
In Turkmenabat, the temperature reached 47.6 degrees; in Samarkand and Fergana, 42.7 degrees; in Syrdarya, 44.6 degrees; in Bishkek, 43.2 degrees; and in Shymkent, 45 degrees. Residents in western Tajikistan are complaining of power cuts, and in recent days residents of Dushanbe and other parts of Tajikistan have also reported frequent power cuts.
Residents of Zarafshon, Chimteppa and districts 33 and 102 of the capital have reported unannounced power cuts on social media, and similar reports have come in from other towns and villages across the country. Residents are reporting overheating and damage to cables in some areas, and the heat is also increasing the use of air conditioners, fridges and fans, putting a strain on the electricity grid. Further measures are being introduced in markets and other crowded areas; in particular, fresh water dispensers are being installed at the Korvon market and in some other open-air commercial areas for traders and visitors.
In Uzbekistan, restrictions on outdoor work are in force; the State Labour Inspectorate has issued an order to all khokimat (mayors) across the country to protect public works employees working outdoors. Work that could pose a threat to employees’ lives and health must be temporarily suspended between 10.00 and 17.00.
Employers are required to introduce flexible working hours, provide employees with drinking water and personal protective equipment, as well as shaded rest areas. Those who have not passed the compulsory medical examinations or who have been deemed unfit for work on health grounds are prohibited from working.
Temporary power cuts have also occurred in some areas of Bishkek and the Chuy region.
According to Kyrgyzstan’s national electricity grid, consumption has reached record levels, and the infrastructure is operating at maximum capacity. In some parts of the capital, water and telecoms services were disrupted, lifts stopped working and traffic lights went out, due to the emergency shutdown of two high-voltage power lines. High temperatures have damaged roads in the Žambyl, Pavlodar and Turkestan regions of Kazakhstan; six areas of thermal deformation on concrete roads have been identified, and road maintenance teams with specialist equipment have been dispatched to carry out repairs.
As previously explained to Asia-Plus by Nawruz Odinaev, head of the Khimoya law firm, employers are obliged to ensure safe and hygienic working conditions: “If an employee discovers a danger to their life and health and is unable to eliminate it, they have the right to stop work. The employee’s average wage is maintained until the hazard has been eliminated,” the lawyer clarified.
According to Jamila Baidullaeva, head of Tajikistan’s Hydrometeorological Centre, temperatures of around 40 degrees in July are normal for the country’s lowland regions, but if such temperatures persist for four or five days, they can be considered abnormal. The expert also explained that air temperatures are measured in the shade; thermometers placed in the sun near hot tarmac or roads may show higher readings and may not reflect the actual air temperature.

France's June heat wave linked to 5,764 excess deaths, highest since 2003
PARIS, Jul 22: France recorded 5,764 excess deaths during the exceptional heat wave that swept across the country between June 17 and July 2, marking the highest excess mortality since the devastating 2003 heat wave, Public Health France said on...
PARIS, Jul 22: France recorded 5,764 excess deaths during the exceptional heat wave that swept across the country between June 17 and July 2, marking the highest excess mortality since the devastating 2003 heat wave, Public Health France said on Wednesday.
The public health agency cautioned that it is not yet possible to definitively attribute the increase in all-cause mortality to the heat, and said the figures remain provisional pending further consolidation in the coming months.
Excess mortality was recorded across all age groups aged 15 and above, with a particularly sharp rise among people aged 45 and older.
The agency described the age pattern as "unprecedented."
The revised estimate includes death certificates that were not captured in earlier assessments, particularly cases involving people who were found dead at home.
The latest figures are significantly higher than an initial estimate released on July 3, when Health Minister Stephanie Rist reported at least 2,025 excess deaths between June 22 and June 28. She had said at the time that the tally was incomplete because authorities were still awaiting death reports and had relied only on electronic death certificates. (UNI)

RAPID CITY, S.D. (KOTA) -
Showers and thunderstorms are possible throughout the overnight hours with low temperatures making it down to average in the lower to middle 60s. Showers remain likely throughout much of the day tomorrow, with a few rumbles of thunder also possible. High temperatures stay on the cooler side tomorrow with some locations not getting out of the upper 70s all day long, Rapid City included. Some spots could see some warmer temperatures, especially the further west you go.
Extreme heat arrives back to KOTA Territory as early as Friday. High temperatures will soar back to the upper 90s by the afternoon with sunshine returning to dominance. Saturday, more sunshine is expected as temperatures will likely make it to the triple digits. The forecasted high of 105 degrees in Rapid City would tie the daily high temperature record, set back two years ago in 2024. Sunday, highs make it back to the triple digits with a forecasted high of 103 in Rapid City. This would get close to the daily high temperature record of 104, set in 1935.
Monday, we continue to see the extreme heat with highs flirting with the triple digits once again. A few isolated thunderstorms are possible to start the work week with dry thunderstorms our main concern, raising the threat for fire weather. That threat continues into Tuesday as temperatures get into the mid to upper 90s. More mid 90s are possible on Wednesday as mostly sunny skies take over.

full_text:
SEOUL, July 23 (Yonhap) -- The ongoing monsoon season is expected to end early next week and be followed by an intense heat wave, the state weather agency said Thursday.
This year's heavy downpours are determined to have already ended on the southern island of Jeju on Sunday, and are expected to end in the central and southern regions around next Monday, the Korea Meteorological Administration (KMA) said.
Starting late next week, a heat wave is forecast to sweep across the nation with morning temperatures ranging between 23 C and 26 C and daytime temperatures ranging from 29 C to 37 C from next Monday through Aug. 2.
The monsoon season started later than usual this year, on June 30 in Jeju and the southern region, and on July 1 in the central region, according to the KMA.
If it ends as forecast, this year's monsoon will have been shorter than the typical year.
The agency, however, noted localized heavy downpours could still occur at any time, with high temperatures and humidity brought by the North Pacific High possibly leading to the sudden development of strong rain clouds.

France’s deadliest heat wave since 2003 killed more than 2,700 people — and possibly as many as 5,700.
More than 5,700 excess deaths were recorded in France during this summer’s record-breaking heat wave, the country’s health agency announced today. That makes the event — which ran, by the official reckoning, from June 17 to July 2 — the country’s deadliest heat wave in more than 20 years.
That’s in line with other estimates we’ve heard. EuroMOMO, a network of European public health agencies that track excess mortality, found that the continent saw more than 10,000 excess deaths during the same period. Roughly 90% of those victims were older than 65, it said. (France’s cohort seems similar: Adults older than 75 made up about two-thirds of the victims, the government said.)
These numbers are staggering — and much larger than some astute Heatmap readers might anticipate. If you read my colleague Jeva Lange’s piece on why it’s so hard to estimate heat deaths last week, she cited a much smaller estimate: Roughly 2,700 died in France during the most recent heat wave. That tally came from Christopher Callahan, an Indiana University scientist who studies climate change’s economic and social costs.
Why is there such a gap between the figures? I emailed Callahan to find out. He shared a few thoughts. First, he uses a different (and theoretically more rigorous) method than the French government: “Our approach uses a statistical relationship between temperature and mortality to explicitly quantify how many additional deaths are associated with a given day’s temperature,” he wrote. “France’s report of excess deaths is just based on how many more people died in late June compared to previous Junes - but we don’t know if those people died because of the heat or some other factor.” (Carbon Brief recently published a Q&A on these varying approaches.)
That might mean his estimate is right, in which case France has misidentified roughly nearly 3,000 deaths. But it could also mean his model, which is trained on data from 2004 to 2019, is “missing something,” he said, like a post-Covid change to public health risk. Last year, Callahan and his colleagues used a similar model to estimate deaths from France’s worst-ever heatwave, a 2003 episode that overwhelmed morgues and killed about 16,000 people. Even 23 years ago, global warming helped make that disaster larger than it needed to be: Some 6,000 of those deaths were due to climate change, their paper found.
Either estimate of the 2026 heat wave, of course, is shattering. As Jeva wrote, even the lower figure would mean the 2026 heat wave killed as many people as died in three years of French homicides. But the divergence in estimates tells us something else too: Even as climate change breaks records and alters our world, we’re never going to quite agree on where it ends and normal randomness begins.
The AI data center boom does not seem close to ending. Google’s parent company, Alphabet, announced its second quarter results this evening, and it beat Wall Street’s expectations, nearly quadrupling its profit on a year-over-year basis. Among the drivers: Its cloud business grew 82% compared to the same quarter last year. (As I’ve written, that rapid growth is helping to turn Alphabet and other hyperscalers into light industrial firms.)
The company’s AI bets seem to be paying off so far — so Google is now planning on spending even more on data centers, energy infrastructure and AI development this year than it once anticipated. It raised its estimates of 2026 capital expenditure to $195 billion to $205 billion, which is above earlier projections and twice as much as it spent in the same category last year. 2027 could be even bigger, it signaled. The company’s shares fell slightly on the news in after-hours trading, but from an energy and climate wonk perspective, the message is clear: For now, the AI demand surge transforming the power sector — and the real economy — continues to chug along.

The spinoff of Lawrence Livermore National Lab has a new 10-point plan to get onto the grid by the 2030s.
One of fusion energy’s newest startups, Inertia Enterprises, is betting that the fastest route to commercial fusion runs through one of the field’s oldest ideas. The company, which raised a $450 million Series A earlier this year, plans to build a power plant based on the laser-driven fusion system pioneered at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s — the only tech yet to have produced more energy from a fusion reaction than it took to initiate it. Now, Inertia has shared its commercialization roadmap exclusively with Heatmap, detailing the 10 near-term capabilities it must demonstrate before this landmark experiment can become a grid-scale power plant by the mid-2030s.
The roadmap offers a route from the national lab’s impressive but commercially impractical fusion demonstrations to an economical power plant capable of producing electricity for the grid. At its core are a set of milestones — mostly aimed at developing cheap, mass-manufacturable components — that Inertia says it must clear before those individual systems can be integrated into a working plant. This road is not necessarily linear, however, as various teams will likely be working on many of these goals simultaneously.
At least the physics of Inertia’s approach are already proven, the startup’s CEO Jeff Lawson told me, pointing to the fusion experiments at Lawrence Livermore’s National Ignition Facility as a proof-of-concept. The lab’s demonstration of net energy gain caps more than six decades and $30 billion (in 2026 dollars) of U.S. fusion research. The remaining challenges, he argued, are all engineering-related, requiring “elbow grease, hard work, and smart people” rather than breakthroughs in fusion science.
"It seems to us like a startup or a commercial company of any variety should be focused on commercializing a proven scientific result, as opposed to actually trying to demonstrate the basic science to begin with," Lawson told me. Basic science, he argues, is better left to national labs and universities, where researchers can pursue "unbounded problems" that don’t align with the expectations and timelines of venture-backed startups.
Indeed, no fusion startup has yet achieved scientific breakeven, the milestone Lawrence Livermore first hit in 2022, and has since repeated numerous times. But leading players such as Commonwealth Fusion Systems and Helion Energy maintain that it’s only a matter of time before they validate the physics behind their own reactor designs, which they claim will be highly cost-competitive.
Lawson, on the other hand, readily acknowledged that Lawrence Livermore’s tech is uneconomical in its current form. His bet is simply that the more predictable path to a commercial reactor is to drive down the cost of the lab’s validated fusion approach, known as inertial confinement. This system relies on high-powered lasers firing at a millimeter-scale pellet of fusion fuel, compressing it to extreme temperatures and pressures until the atoms fuse. Today, the National Ignition Facility makes each individual fusion target by hand, a workable solution given that it only uses about a dozen per year.
That production model, however, isn’t remotely plausible for a grid-scale power plant. Because each fusion reaction lasts just a fraction of a billionth of a second, a commercial facility must fire its lasers at a fresh target about 10 times per second to generate continuous electricity — requiring the production of hundreds of millions of targets each year.
Scaling production to roughly a million pellets per day and making them inexpensive enough for commercial operation without compromising the strength or precision required for fusion ignition is central to Inertia’s roadmap. That includes goals five, seven, eight and nine — industrializing the manufacturing of the carbon shells that hold the fusion fuel, making the thin films that hold those carbon shells both durable and cheap, scaling up and automating fusion target assembly, and speeding up how fast targets are filled with the requisite deuterium-tritium fuel.
The other central focus of the roadmap is the laser system, which will ultimately consist of 1,000 individual units operating in concert to compress and heat the fusion fuel. Key priorities include reducing the system’s cost (goal two), dramatically increasing its firing cadence (goal three), and bolstering its durability to withstand high-intensity operations (goal four). Goal six also complements these efforts, calling for the development of a control system capable of tracking moving fusion targets to precisely align each laser shot.
Goals one and 10 bookend the journey with some broader milestones. The first focuses on increasing the fusion target’s energy gain — the ratio of fusion energy produced to laser energy delivered — to more than 25 times ignition. Today, the National Ignition Facility’s best-performing laser shot has yielded a gain of just over four times what it took to start the reaction. Goal 10 then zooms out to the ultimate objective: integrating all these technologies into a commercially viable power plant that can deliver either electricity or industrial heat to end customers.
To reach that point, Inertia has embarked on an industrial engineering hiring spree, recruiting folks with experience taking complex hardware systems from prototype to mass production, “not unlike the processes that are used in the semiconductor or consumer electronics world,” Lawson explained. The company has been making progress on its component development goals since the beginning of the year, he told me, and expects to announce the successful demonstration of a few of these milestones in the coming months. Lawson ultimately expects Inertia to complete the core components of its laser and target manufacturing systems by the middle of next year.
The team will spend the next two to three years integrating these individual pieces into two fully operational subsystems, a prototype laser system and a target manufacturing line. Around 2030, the company will begin combining those subsystems into a first-of-a-kind fusion power plant, which will also serve as the proving ground for the target chamber, tritium fuel breeding system, and power conversion system that turns fusion heat into electricity. By the middle of the next decade, Inertia aims to be generating power from this first plant, setting the stage for the company to build and connect additional grid-scale commercial power plants.
There are plenty of engineering trade-offs that the company will have to solve for. Take the decision around how to size the target chamber, for example. “If you make it bigger, your walls have an easier time and survive longer, but it’s more expensive. If you make it smaller, your walls have a tougher time because they’re closer to all the heat and energy that the fusion reaction is creating, but now your power plant costs less to build.”
But to Lawson, this represents exactly the type of problem Inertia was built to solve: complex engineering issues that come to the fore once scientists have demonstrated the fundamental physics are sound. He thinks other fusion companies may someday reach this stage, as well — though he’s unwilling to hazard a guess on exactly what approach or startup is best positioned to do so.
“There have been generations of scientists who’ve made their predictions about fusion energy and gotten it wrong,” he told me. “I’m not going to pretend to be smarter than them. All I’m here to say is, just knowing that one did work, we can commercialize it.”

title: Dehydrated hedgehogs: Warnings issues by West wildlife centres - BBC
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
- Published
Staff at a hedgehog rescue centre say they are at full capacity treating dozens of dehydrated animals because of months of intense heat.
Yvonne Cox, co-founder of Hedgehog Rescue in Yate near Bristol, said the animals have been "coming in thick and fast", with about 40 mothers and babies currently being cared for in the centre.
Cox said the creatures have been struggling to find food and drink in the hot dry weather, and that the issue has been exacerbated due to it being the peak of baby season.
"We're having a lot of hoglets coming in that seem to be separated from mum, or maybe mum has perished," she said.
Cox listed warning signs to help people identify a struggling hedgehog, including seeing them wandering around in the day looking lethargic and wobbly.
"They're nocturnal animals, but in this extreme heat, they may leave the nest because the nest site might just get too warm," said Cox.
Other warning signs include finding a hedgehog lying on the floor or seeing flies buzzing around it.
More from Bristol
Lauren Valentine, wildlife care and veterinary services manager at Secret World Wildfire Rescue in East Huntspill, near Highbridge in Somerset, said summer is the busiest time for staff.
Like Cox, she said the recent hot weather had "exacerbated" the number of cases at the centre.
She added that their hedgehog wing - named Hogwarts - had moved to a temporary unit as the original wing is too hot and the air conditioner unit had broken.
Amid the high temperatures, staff have treated animals suffering with dehydration and disorientation caused by the heat, as well as those with injuries, Valentine said.
"It snowballs... if they're disorientated, they're more likely to be found by a predator.
"They can't evade that predator because they're dehydrated and not feeling too well, so it all compounds," she said.
Members of the public are encouraged to leave water out for hedgehogs and other wildlife who may struggle in the heat and urged to report animals in need of help to wildlife centres where they can get professional care.
Some parts of the UK have experienced hotter temperatures than normal during the first part of July, with parts of the south-west England exceeding 30C (86F) for ten days.
Stuart McCarthy-Thompson from Avon Wildlife Trust said the prolonged dry weather has made life difficult for hedgehogs - a species which has been in decline for years.
"The ground is very dry, making it hard to find their usual food such as beetles and worms, and natural sources of water are in short supply," he said.
Grace Johnson from the People's Trust for Endangered Species said placing shallow dishes of clean, fresh water in the garden can help hedgehogs and other wildlife.
"Allowing sections of the garden to be wild and overgrown can also help by offering shade and shelter," she said.
McCarthy-Thompson said you can also support hedgehogs by providing meaty dog or cat food along with the water.

title: Africa's camels are dying from extreme heat as temperatures soar - BBC
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Andaman and Nicobar Islands
full_text:
- Published
Camels are famously known as the ships of the desert, but even these hardy animals are not being spared from the extremes of rising temperatures in Africa.
The Afar region of north-eastern Ethiopia is one of the hottest and driest places on Earth, where nomads often lead camel caravans carrying salt through the desert.
"I can see my camels suffering from extreme heat," Ali Umer, a camel herder from Semera in Afar, told the BBC.
"They get blisters on their feet, their eyes become watery and the hot sand burns their skin and eats up their hair when they are sitting," said the 40-year-old who owns around 500 camels.
"Within the past one month I have lost eight camel calves because of the extreme heat, it has become much more intense and difficult.
"Even the wind, which used to bring cool air, now carries heat," he said, adding he had noticed these drastic changes in the temperature in recent years.
He is not alone in his observations: scientists, animal welfare workers and various studies all mention stress, fatigue, dehydration, disease prevalence as direct impacts of extreme heat.
The higher temperatures also indirectly make camels suffer by causing severe loss of water, vegetation, and reduced shaded areas.
North Africa and the Horn of Africa host around 80% of the world's largest population of dromedary camels - those with one hump.
Over the years, farmers and cattle herders in these regions have switched to these animals because of their resilience to harsh heat and drought conditions.
But a study based on the perception of camel herders in southern Ethiopia, published in 2025, , externalshowed that high temperatures and climate variability had had a detrimental impact on camel herd health.
"Respondents reported that climate change and variability have affected camels' health, productivity, and reproductive performance," it said.
In neighbouring Somalia, the situation appears to be more serious. Research published in June, external has shown that drought-related camel mortality is a widespread crisis, affecting nearly 46% of camel-owning households.
The study included nearly 6,000 pastoral households and showed that the mortality was concentrated in northern regions, with Sool reporting a "staggering mortality rate of nearly 73%".
Extreme heat is the main cause behind the drought in the region although other factors like inadequate veterinary services and animal management make things worse, experts say.
An analysis by atmospheric scientists, external found that high temperatures in the region were behind the 2021-22 drought, as extreme heat makes water from soil and water bodies turn into vapour and plants release water in them into air as vapour.
Veterinarians say they are treating more sick camels and are seeing more deaths due to extreme heat and drought.
"We are seeing significant increase in cases of hyperthermia in camels as a result of which they get multiple infections," Hassan Nuya Guyo, a veterinary officer in Marsabit county in north-eastern Kenya bordering Ethiopia, told the BBC.
"In the last two years, we have seen a more than 15% increase in camel deaths in our county due to extreme heat."
Disease prevalence among camels and reduced milk yield from them are as a result of climate change impacts including heat stress, according to another study conducted in the Horn of Africa, external.
A similar story is playing out in North Africa. A study in Algeria, external, published this year, has shown a significant increase in camel deaths during summer heatwaves.
"Time series analysis highlights significant seasonal mortality patterns, underscoring the need for targeted interventions during high-risk periods like peak summer months; such as strategic water provisioning, supplemental feeding, and enhanced disease monitoring," it said.
The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) says extreme heat events have become a common phenomenon in several North African countries with heatwaves having increased with a clear upward trend since 1981.
It said a number of countries in the region and the Middle East reported temperatures above 50C in 2024.
WMO's State of the Climate in Africa 2025 report, external said North Africa recorded the most rapid warming of the six subregions - 0.43C per decade - between 1991 and 2025.
Experts say camels comfortably beat the heat up to around 40C by mainly allowing their body temperature to fluctuate by several degrees throughout the day to deal with outside temperature.
By doing that they avoid sweating and, combined with concentrated urine and dry faeces, it helps them minimise water loss, while their thick skin coat helps them ward off solar radiation.
"But camels can also experience heat stress when temperatures rise beyond their comfort range," said Dr Mohammed Bengoumi, a scientist with the UN's Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), who specialises in camel health and works in North Africa.
"In many parts of the Sahara, summer temperatures now frequently exceed 50C, increasing the risk of severe heat stress, particularly when accompanied by reduced vegetation cover, water scarcity, and limited access to shade," he told the BBC.
Professor Abdelmadjid Chehma at the University of Ghardaia in Algeria, who has conducted experimental works on camels in the Sahara Desert, has a similar conclusion.
"Our works show that prolonged day-and-night heat above 41 to 45C directly attack their cells," he told the BBC.
"Their immune system suffers because such heat levels damage their white blood cells and other cells because of stress, they [the cells] struggle to repair themselves and end up destroying themselves."
For camels this means when heat stress sets in, their metabolism slows down to limit internal heat production leading to sharp reduction in food intake resulting in lethargy and generalised fatigue.
That leads to drop in milk production and also causes reproductive disorders.
Professor Mohammed el Khasmi, animal hydrobiology expert at the University of Casablanca in Morocco, has a long list of effects of heat stress he and his team have observed on camels.
Weight loss, inflammation, muscle and bone related illnesses, chemical imbalance due to kidney malfunction, infectious diseases caused by parasites were a few among them, he said.
While extreme heat is the primary culprit, its impacts become severe when other factors like loss of vegetation and water, lack of shaded areas and inadequate veterinary services add up, experts say.
This is why many herders in Ethiopia have moved their camels from the northern region, where the animals were found historically, to the south where water and vegetation are relatively available, experts there say.
"And this is not the seasonal movement of the herders," said Daniel Gelan, professor and pastoralist development researcher with Vision University in Ethiopia.
"They are leaving the northern region for good because they can no longer sustain camel herding there because of extreme heat and drought," he told the BBC.
The Horn of Africa has been recovering from the longest and most severe recorded drought spanning from 2020 to 2023, but now it is seeing another round of the harsh condition, according to charity organisation Oxfam.
It said water prices in some parts of Somalia had risen by more than 2,000% because of the drought.
Umer, the cattle herder in Afar, said he was worried that an outbreak of disease could start killing his adult camels anytime now.
His concern is valid, according to Prof Chehma.
"While camels remain the livestock herders' last line of defence against the desert, the unprecedented intensity of current heatwaves directly threatens their vital functions, turning this historically resilient animal into a climate victim," he said.
Additional reporting by BBC Amharic's Firehiwot Kassa

Italy battles 3rd major heatwave of summer
Published : 23 Jul 2026, 03:31
Italy is grappling with its third major heatwave of the summer, with soaring temperatures triggering nationwide health alerts, fueling wildfires and prompting authorities to step up emergency measures as scientists warn that climate change is intensifying extreme weather, reported Xinhua.
On Tuesday, the Italian Health Ministry placed 18 major cities, including Rome, Florence, Naples, Palermo and Catania, under its highest-level "red alert" for extreme heat.
The warning indicates conditions that pose health risks not only to vulnerable groups but also to healthy people exposed to prolonged high temperatures. While temperatures are expected to ease in parts of the country later this week, southern regions are forecast to remain under intense heat.
The prolonged hot and dry conditions have sharply increased wildfire risks, particularly in southern Italy.
In Sicily, more than 100 residents were evacuated after a wildfire threatened the town of Santo Stefano Quisquina near Agrigento. Strong sirocco winds fueled the blaze, endangering an oxygen storage facility, a medical clinic, a fuel station and a fireworks factory. "The situation is dramatic," Mayor Francesco Cacciatore said, urging residents in affected areas to evacuate.
The wildfire also forced the temporary closure of part of the Corleonese-Agrigentina state highway, while another blaze burned through Mediterranean vegetation near Agrigento overnight.
Scientists say longer droughts, rising temperatures and stronger winds associated with climate change have substantially increased the frequency and intensity of wildfires, which have become an increasingly common feature of Italian summers.
The latest heatwave has also increased pressure on agriculture, with farmers reporting growing concerns over water shortages, heat stress on livestock and declining crop yields.
The Italian government has stepped up nationwide emergency measures to reduce health risks.
The Health Ministry has activated its national heat emergency plan, continued issuing daily heat bulletins covering 27 major cities, and coordinated with regional authorities and the Civil Protection Department. Authorities have also advised people to avoid outdoor activities during the hottest hours, stay hydrated, and check on elderly relatives and neighbors.
Several regions have maintained restrictions on outdoor work in sectors such as construction and agriculture during the hottest part of the day. Italy also continues to use the Worklimate early-warning system, jointly developed by the National Research Council (CNR) and the National Institute for Insurance against Accidents at Work (INAIL), to help protect workers from heat stress.
Massimiliano Pasqui, a senior researcher at the Institute for BioEconomy of the National Research Council, told local media that the current heatwave is being driven by a persistent "omega" high-pressure pattern that traps hot air from North Africa over Italy for several days.
He said such prolonged heatwaves are becoming more frequent as global temperatures rise, increasing the risks of drought, wildfires and health emergencies.
Scientists say that although heatwaves are a familiar feature of Mediterranean summers, climate change is making them more frequent, longer-lasting and more intense, underscoring the need for stronger emergency preparedness and long-term climate adaptation measures.

Wildfires raged on Wednesday in Sicily, southwestern France and central Spain, having claimed the lives of three firefighters and sparked thousands of evacuations.
Temperatures topped 40 degrees Celsius and gusts of hot wind fanned the flames in the southern Italian island, where wildfires have been raging for several days.

title: Tokyo reports most heat-related hospitalisations since 2010, one dead - CNA

full_text:
TOKYO: More than 450 people received hospital treatment for heat-related issues in a single day in Tokyo - the highest number since records began 16 years ago, emergency services said on Thursday (Jul 23).
The figure was released as Japan sweltered in a heatwave that has killed at least 14 people over the past week.
A total 453 people had hospital treatment for heat-related issues on Wednesday, the most since records began in 2010, the Tokyo Fire Department said. Emergency callouts were also at the highest level since at least 1963.
"We are urging people to stay hydrated and use air conditioners," a fire brigade spokesman told AFP.
The number of patients appeared to rise as temperatures climbed, with notable increases on Tuesday and Wednesday, the spokesman said.
In Tokyo prefecture - home to around 14 million people - one person had died in the current heatwave with four others in a critical condition and 18 in a serious state, he said.
Nick Estes, 35, an American tourist visiting the Asakusa area of Tokyo with his family, said Thursday the weather was "extremely hot".
"We've been planning our trips around trying to make sure we can spend time in the metro to cool off, finding little shops to pop into to maybe buy a little souvenir that has air conditioning, doing everything we can to stay cool," Estes told AFP.
"But especially with younger kids, it's been absolutely brutal ... I was here last summer, actually, and somehow it actually feels even hotter this year than it did a year ago."
The Fire and Disaster Management Agency has dubbed the heatwave a "disaster", reporting on Wednesday at least 14 deaths across the country over the previous week and 11,000 people seeking emergency medical care.
"DISASTER"
In the central region of Aichi, which includes Nagoya and is experiencing some of the most intense heat, 116 people were taken to hospital on Wednesday, officials said.
Heatstroke alerts have been issued for almost the whole country of around 125 million people.
"Temperatures have been rising since mid-July, and severe heat is expected to continue nationwide," the disaster agency said.
"It is no exaggeration to call this extreme heat a 'disaster' with many people being rushed to hospitals or even dying as a result of heatstroke," it said.
On Thursday, at least six places experienced 40°C temperatures or hotter by early afternoon, while the mercury rose to 39.9°C in three other locations.
The hottest place was Hamamatsu in the central Shizuoka region at 41.1°C.
It was followed by Toyota city - headquarters of the car giant - in the neighbouring Aichi region at 40.8°C and Kuwana in Mie at 40.7°C.
Japan's highest recorded temperature was registered on Aug 5, 2025, at 41.8°C in Isesaki, north of Tokyo.
Japan introduced this year a new term to describe heat above 40C called "kokushobi", which translates to "cruelly hot day".
The new term was designed to draw greater public attention to heat-related warnings, although it does not trigger any official measures nationwide.
"HOTTER AND HOTTER"
Scientists say human-induced climate change is increasing the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events in Japan and elsewhere.
Japan sweltered through its hottest summer on record last year.
"You leave the hotel at 8.30 ... it feels as if it was noontime. And as the day progresses, it just gets hotter and hotter and hotter," said Yesenia Gueta, 47, visiting from Los Angeles.
"Now I understand why (Japanese) clothing is loose, why they wear the umbrellas all the time. I mean, it is extreme. And I agree with that term," Gueta said.
"Since it's like this in mid-July, I wonder what's going to happen in August. I'm worried," said Japanese university student Nukochama, 24.
"I carry a big fan like this, fully charged, so I can turn it on anytime."

full_text:
FRESNO, Calif. (KFSN) -- As summer temperatures rise, local doctors are urging families to take precautions and be aware of the serious impact extreme heat can have on heart health, particularly for older adults and people living with cardiovascular disease.
Medical experts say the heart must work harder to keep the body cool during hot weather. According to Dignity Health Mercy Medical Center, that added workload can become dangerous for people with underlying heart conditions.
"There have been some reports of excessive heat leading to over 2,000 additional deaths per year," said Dr. Rajiv Maraj, an interventional cardiologist.
Health experts say the added stress that hot weather places on the heart can increase the risk of heat-related illness, heart attack, stroke and other cardiovascular complications among vulnerable individuals.
"Yeah, so 65 and older, they are definitely more vulnerable in the hot weather," said Dr. Vivian Torio, a Kaiser Permanente family medicine physician.
Dr. Maraj said older adults with existing heart conditions can face heightened risks during periods of extreme heat.
"Elderly patients, they have issues with heart disease, heart failure. They may have had blockages in the blood vessels to the heart, and they've had stents, bypass surgery, so those patients are very vulnerable," he said.
Dr. Maraj said excessive strain on the heart can worsen existing medical conditions.
"Excess workload on the heart could cause chest pain. It could make or exacerbate heart failure worse," he said.
Dr. Torio said dehydration can also cause noticeable symptoms in older adults.
"Look out for them being more tired, more sleepy, confused. They may complain of feeling dizzy, their heart racing," she said.
Doctors recommend several steps to reduce heat-related stress on the heart.
They advise avoiding outdoor activities during extreme temperatures, especially between noon and 3 pm, avoiding beverages containing caffeine and alcohol while drinking plenty of water, seeking shade and wearing loose, light-colored clothing and a wide-brim hat; and regularly checking in on older adults and loved ones.
For those who want to remain active during hot weather, Dr. Torio said exercise can still be done safely by adjusting the time and location.
"Go on your walk early in the morning or later in the evening after the sun goes down, even exercising in your home," she said. "YouTube is great. There are a lot of exercise programs you can do, especially for seniors."
Healthcare experts say taking precautions to stay cool and hydrated can help reduce strain on the heart during periods of extreme heat.

Manna seeks water for homeless in PQB
Published 8:30 pm Wednesday, July 22, 2026
The Manna Homeless Society is calling on Oceanside residents, businesses and community organizations to donate bottled water as summer temperatures rise and the risk of heat-related illness increases for people experiencing homelessness and housing insecurity.
The society says its outreach teams are seeing growing numbers of seniors and other vulnerable adults struggling to cope with the heat while living outdoors or in precarious housing. To help prevent dehydration and heat-related illnesses, volunteers are asking for donations of sealed cases of bottled water that can be distributed during street outreach throughout the summer.
Robin Campbell of the Manna Homeless Society said access to clean drinking water is one of the simplest and most effective ways to protect people during periods of extreme heat.
According to the society, dehydration can develop quickly during hot weather as the body loses fluids through perspiration. Symptoms such as dizziness, confusion, muscle cramps and a rapid heartbeat can worsen if left untreated, potentially leading to heat exhaustion or life-threatening heat stroke.
Seniors are particularly vulnerable because their sense of thirst often decreases with age, while chronic health conditions and certain medications can increase the risk of dehydration.
The organization said providing bottled water during outreach not only helps people stay hydrated and regulate their body temperature, but may also reduce preventable visits to walk-in clinics and hospital emergency departments, allowing limited community resources to be focused on other essential supports such as meals, hygiene supplies and housing assistance.
Residents wishing to help can drop off sealed cases of bottled water at Manna Homeless Society’s location at 1240 Leffler Rd. in Errington. Those with larger donations can arrange for pickup by contacting the society at Oceansidemanna@gmail.com.
The organization is also encouraging residents to share the appeal with workplaces, churches, service clubs and neighbours, noting that every donation, no matter the size, will help keep vulnerable members of the community safe during the summer months.

Amidst clear skies, continuously high temperatures are again set to be observed across Macau over the next few days, with the Meteorological Bureau (SMG) yesterday issuing its Yellow hot weather alert.
Temperatures are expected to go up to 33 degrees Celsius or higher, the bureau said, with members of the public reminded to be wary of heatstroke.
With the issuance of the Yellow hot weather alert, the government’s heat shelter is open once again, as noted on the Social Welfare Bureau (IAS) website yesterday, adding that it opens its doors free of charge when a Yellow or Orange hot weather alert is up.
The Health Bureau (SSM) meanwhile reminded the public that extreme heat combined with high humidity, and poor ventilation can affect the body’s natural ability to effectively cool itself, with heat-related illnesses possibly setting in.
Among these are heat syncope, where dilated blood vessels reduce blood flow to the brain and other parts of the body, causing fainting, weak pulse, and moist/cool skin. Sweat-driven salt loss meanwhile leads to heat cramps and muscle spasms.
Continued fluid and salt loss causes heat exhaustion, which includes symptoms like extreme thirst, fatigue, limb weakness, nausea, headache, dizziness, temporary clouding of consciousness, cool and clammy skin, pale complexion, and a rapid yet weak pulse, according to the statement.
The statement added that heatstroke, upon prolonged exposure to high temperatures, affects the central nervous system, causing the body to lose its temperature-regulating capability. The statement said that sweating either fails or stops; body temperatures can hit 41°C and, if victims are unable to cool down and receive emergency care, it leads to irreversible brain damage or death.
Among the bureau’s reminders are avoiding outdoor activities in sweltering heat; steering clear of strenuous activities in hot and humid environments or ensuring good ventilation in these types of areas; replenishing fluids lost through sweating by drinking electrolyte-containing beverages rather than caffeinated drinks or alcoholic beverages.
Other reminders included using sun protection gear or sun safe attire while engaging in outdoor activities and not staying in cars parked outdoors for long periods. Lastly, the statement emphasised that the obese, elderly, young children, pregnant and postpartum women, or those suffering from illnesses “should pay special attention to keeping their environment well-ventilated and at a suitable temperature, avoiding prolonged exposure to direct sunlight”.
Anyone feeling unwell is remind to seek immediate medical attention, the statement pointed out, and, in severe cases, one should call 999 to be taken by ambulance to hospital.
Meanwhile, as noted in a Post article yesterday, a low-pressure system located east of the Philippines may develop into a tropical cyclone, being expected to move towards the northern South China Sea in the next few days. It might intensify into a typhoon. Consequently, Macau may experience significantly stronger winds, heavy showers, and occasional thunderstorms over the weekend.
At 1 a.m. today, the mercury stood at 28°C, with relative humidity at 91 percent, according to the local observatory, which warned that the apparent temperature “feels hot”.

WorkSafeBC warns employers to protect workers from heat stress, heat stroke
Published 11:00 am Wednesday, July 22, 2026
WorkSafeBC has issued a bulletin to remind employers that they must protect their workers from heat-related illnesses as Environment Canada issues heat alerts.
This applies to staffers working inside and outside.
Todd McDonald, WorkSafeBC’s head of prevention services, warned that higher temperatures by nature increase the risk of heat stress and heat stroke, “which can be serious and even life-threatening.
“These illnesses are preventable, and employers have a responsibility to take steps to protect workers, whether they are indoors or outdoors,” McDonald said.
WorkSafeBC received 335 heat-related injury claims between 2021 and 2025, with 47 of them in 2025.
The July 21 bulletin notes those working in construction, agriculture, transportation, and public works are at higher risk but people working indoors — such as in kitchens, warehouses, and manufacturing buildings — are also susceptible, particularly if there’s no air conditioning.
WorkSafeBC requires employers to conduct risk assessments and develop safety plans.
Accordingly, they should provide cool drinking water and regular rest breaks, set up shady or cooled recovery areas, schedule strenuous task during cooler times in the day, improve ventilation, and rotate workers between hotter and cooler environments.
Employers also need to train their workers to recognize early signs of heat stress and how to access first aid. The bulletin notes that symptoms can include excessive sweating, muscle cramps, nausea, weakness, dizziness, agitation and confusion.
Workers should report symptoms and be encouraged to speak up if things are not safe.
Employees during hot weather should also drink lots of water – a glass every 20 minutes – wear sunscreen, wear light breathable clothing like cotton, be aware of their personal risks related to medications or health conditions, and watch for signs of heat stress in themselves and co-workers.
Anyone with questions about heat stress can call 604-276-3100 or toll-free at 1-888-621-7233 for information.

Rain red alert: Intense monsoon to flood Gujarat, heavy downpour to hit Rajasthan
The IMD has issued a red alert for several Gujarat districts as a fresh monsoon surge intensifies rainfall across western India. The active system is set to spread heavy showers into Rajasthan, raising flooding risks while easing recent humid conditions.
A fresh surge in monsoon activity is set to unleash widespread heavy rainfall across western India, with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) issuing a red alert for several districts of Gujarat as an intensifying weather system moves steadily westward.
According to the IMD's latest nowcast, districts including Vadodara, Bharuch, Chhota Udepur, Narmada, Surat, Tapi, Navsari, Dang, Valsad, Ahmedabad, Anand, Bhavnagar, Botad and Panchmahal are likely to witness heavy rain accompanied by thunderstorms, lightning and wind speeds of 41-61 kmph over the next few hours.
Authorities have warned of waterlogging, traffic disruptions, falling trees and an increased risk of lightning strikes.
The alert comes as a monsoon cyclonic circulation over western Madhya Pradesh has intensified, providing abundant moisture and instability across central and western India.
Meteorologists said the system is expected to continue triggering intense thunderstorms over western Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, southeast Rajasthan and eastern Gujarat, before gradually shifting westward over the coming days.
As the circulation progresses, rainfall activity is forecast to expand further into Rajasthan. Forecasters said the next two to four days will likely bring the most-awaited spell of monsoon thunderstorms to southwest Rajasthan, a region that has been waiting for a significant rainfall event this season.
South Rajasthan, including districts bordering Gujarat, is expected to receive widespread moderate to heavy showers, with isolated locations likely to witness very heavy rainfall.
The increased precipitation is expected to provide much-needed relief from recent hot and humid conditions while significantly improving seasonal rainfall totals.
Satellite imagery from INSAT-3DS shows towering convective cloud bands stretching from western Madhya Pradesh into Gujarat, with dense cloud clusters building over the Arabian Sea and adjoining land areas.
The satellite observations indicate that the atmosphere remains highly favourable for deep thunderstorm development across western India.
The strengthening monsoon pulse is also expected to enhance rainfall over parts of Maharashtra, while central India continues to remain under the influence of the cyclonic circulation.
The IMD has advised residents in the red alert districts to remain indoors during periods of intense thunderstorms, avoid taking shelter under trees, stay away from open fields and water bodies, and monitor official weather updates.
With the weather system expected to remain active through the weekend, Gujarat and Rajasthan are likely to witness one of their strongest monsoon spells of the season, bringing the potential for both widespread rainfall and localised flooding.

MUMBAI: Mumbai remained under an orange alert for the third consecutive day on Thursday, with the India Meteorological Department (IMD) forecasting heavy to very heavy rainfall at a few places along with gusty winds of 60-70kmph.
Neighbouring Palghar continued under a red alert, while Thane also remained under an orange alert.
Despite the warning, rainfall within Mumbai was moderate compared with other parts of the Mumbai Metropolitan Region (MMR).
In the 24 hours ending 8.30am Thursday, the IMD's Santacruz observatory recorded 63.6 mm of rainfall, while Colaba received 22.2mm. Thane recorded 99.32mm and Navi Mumbai 56.87mm.
The heaviest rainfall was reported from Palghar district, where several areas received triple-digit rainfall.
Jawhar topped the chart with 274mm, followed by Palghar (254mm), Wada (166mm) and Vasai (102mm) in the 24 hours ending Thursday morning. The IMD has forecast extremely heavy rainfall at isolated places in the district.
Within Vasai taluka, Agashi (Virar West) recorded 123mm, Bolinj (Virar) 104mm, Kaman 101mm, Manikpur (Vasai West) 98mm, Mandvi 74mm, and Pelhar (Vasai East) 70mm.
The fresh spell of rainfall significantly boosted water reserves in the seven lakes supplying Mumbai, with live storage rising to 70% of capacity, an increase of nearly 8% points in a single day.
Modak Sagar became the fourth lake to overflow this monsoon after reaching its full supply level at around 2am on Thursday.
The lake supplies 440 million litres per day (MLD) of water to Mumbai.
Mumbai receives 4,100 million litres of water per day (MLD) from seven lakes, Modak Sagar, Bhatsa, Upper Vaitarna, Middle Vaitarna, Vihar, Tulsi and Tansa.
To meet the city’s annual water requirement, the reservoirs need a combined storage of 14.47 lakh million litres.

full_text:
Khadakwasla nears overflow as heavy rain lashes ghats; IMD retains red alert
Pune:The second active spell of the southwest monsoon has intensified across the western ghats; bringing heavy to very heavy rainfall to Pune’s ghat areas over the past two days, and causing the Khadakwasla dam stock to once again cross the 80% mark
Pune:
The second active spell of the southwest monsoon has intensified across the western ghats; bringing heavy to very heavy rainfall to Pune’s ghat areas over the past two days, and causing the Khadakwasla dam stock to once again cross the 80% mark. Whereas Pune city has witnessed intermittent rain from Tuesday evening, with light showers continuing through Wednesday. According to India Meteorological Department (IMD) data, Shivajinagar recorded 7.1 mm rainfall till 5.30 pm; while Pashan received 8.6 mm; Chinchwad 13.5 mm; and Lohegaon 3.8 mm.
S D Sanap, senior meteorologist at IMD Pune, said that active monsoon conditions are expected to continue for the next two to three days, with heavy rainfall likely over Pune’s ghat areas before the intensity gradually eases. “The IMD has issued a red alert for heavy to very heavy rainfall over the ghat sections of Pune on July 23 and an orange Alert for July 24. Pune city is not under any significant warning, although cloudy conditions with light rain are likely over the next two days,” Sanap said. He advised commuters travelling through the ghat sections to exercise caution as intense spells of rain could reduce visibility and trigger landslides, rockfalls and waterlogging. Authorities have also appealed to tourists to stay away from overflowing waterfalls, swollen streams and other vulnerable locations until the weather improves.
Rainfall data for the 24-hour period ending 8.30 am on Wednesday showed exceptionally heavy rain across the Sahyadri belt. Bhira in Mulshi tehsil recorded the highest rainfall (309 mm), followed by Tamhini (275 mm), Davdi (264 mm), Shirgaon (255 mm), Dongerwadi (197 mm), and Ambone (185 mm). Within Pune district, the Mulshi-Tamhini belt remained among the wettest regions. Mulshi Camp recorded 151 mm of rainfall; while Lonavla office received 142 mm; Walvan 123 mm; and Lonavala (Tata) 106 mm during the same period. The cumulative rainfall this monsoon has also been exceptionally high across the western ghats. Bhira has received 5,386 mm so far this season; followed by Tamhini (4,239 mm); Davdi (3,714 mm); Shirgaon (3,568 mm); and Dongerwadi (3,525 mm), reflecting sustained monsoon activity in the region. Meanwhile, persistent rainfall has boosted inflows into reservoirs supplying Pune and neighbouring districts, while streams and waterfalls across the Sahyadris are flowing at full capacity. With monsoon activity over Maharashtra growing stronger, the IMD has retained a red alert for Pune’s ghat sections on Thursday.
Meteorologists have attributed the active spell to a combination of favourable synoptic systems. The monsoon trough remains south of its normal position, strengthening the monsoon flow over central India. An upper-air cyclonic circulation over north Madhya Pradesh, extending up to the middle troposphere; and an east-west shear zone along 22°N are enhancing moisture incursion from the Arabian Sea, leading to widespread rainfall over Maharashtra. A western disturbance over Jammu and Kashmir and another upper-air cyclonic circulation over northeast Assam are also influencing the country’s weather pattern.
Khadakwasla dam crosses 80% storage, again; irrigation dept. warns of possible water release
Continuous heavy rainfall in the catchment areas has led to a sharp increase in water storage across the Khadakwasla dam complex, prompting the irrigation department to issue a precautionary advisory.
By 5 pm on Wednesday, the combined live storage in the four reservoirs — Khadakwasla, Panshet, Varasgaon and Temghar — had reached 20.16 TMC (69.17%); up from 18.31 TMC (62.80%) recorded 24 hours earlier.
Khadakwasla dam witnessed one of the steepest rises, with storage rising from 1.28 TMC (64.75%) Monday evening to 1.56 TMC (79.11%) by 5 pm on Wednesday. By around 7 pm, the reservoir had crossed 80% of its live storage capacity again, prompting officials to warn that spillway gates may have to be opened depending on further rainfall and inflows.
The irrigation department has advised residents living along the Mutha river to remain alert, avoid entering the riverbed, and shift livestock and belongings from low-lying areas, as controlled releases from the dam may become necessary if the heavy rain continues.
